findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting is a hub package with 14 modules and high cross-module coupling inside the layer
evidence: dashboard_generator.py imports from executive_reporter, manuscript_overview, and output_organizer — three distinct sibling modules — in addition to core

multi_project_reporter.py imports generate_all_dashboards from dashboard_generator AND generate_executive_summary/save_executive_summary from executive_reporter, creating an orchestrator that depends on two separate sibling reporting modules

The reporting package has 14 .py files; the cross-import density within a single flat package raises blast radius for any shared type change (e.g., ExecutiveSummary, ProjectMetrics from executive_reporter are re-consumed by dashboard_generator)
findings.suggestion: Introduce a sub-package boundary (e.g., infrastructure/reporting/executive/ vs infrastructure/reporting/pipeline/) so that cross-module edges flow in one direction rather than forming a flat mesh. dashboard_generator should not import from manuscript_overview if those are independent reporting concerns.

findings.summary: logging_utils.py re-imports from five sibling split modules, creating a hub that negates the benefit of the split
evidence: Lines 24-28 of logging_utils.py: imports JSONFormatter, TemplateFormatter, format_duration, format_error_with_suggestions, calculate_eta, psutil, EMOJIS, USE_EMOJIS, USE_STRUCTURED_LOGGING from five different sibling modules
All callers (pipeline.py, environment.py, scripts/01_run_tests.py) import from logging_utils — so logging_utils is still the hub with the same blast radius as before the split
scripts/01_run_tests.py line 38 also directly imports format_duration from infrastructure.core.logging_helpers, bypassing the hub — inconsistent import path for the same symbol
findings.suggestion: Either keep the logging module unsplit (single authoritative file) or fully commit to consumers importing directly from the leaf modules. The current arrangement adds indirection without reducing coupling.

findings.summary: format_duration is importable via two different module paths, creating an inconsistent convention across sibling consumer modules
evidence: pipeline_reporter.py imports format_duration from infrastructure.core.logging_utils (line 16)
scripts/01_run_tests.py imports format_duration as _format_duration from infrastructure.core.logging_helpers (line 38)
Both refer to the same utility function; the aliased import in the script uses a leading underscore convention not used anywhere else
findings.suggestion: Pick one canonical import path (logging_utils or logging_helpers) and enforce it across all consumers. Remove the re-export from whichever module is not the owner.

findings.summary: Some modules use typing.Dict/List (deprecated since 3.9) while others use built-in dict/list generics, inconsistently across sibling files
evidence: multi_project_reporter.py line 11: 'from typing import Any, Dict, List' then uses Dict[str, Path], List[Any] throughout
pipeline_reporter.py line 13: 'from typing import Any, Optional' then uses list[StageResult], dict[str, Any] (built-in lowercase)
dashboard_generator.py line 12: 'from typing import Any, Dict, List' mixed usage
These are sibling modules in the same package using two different annotation styles
findings.suggestion: Standardize on the lowercase built-in generics (dict, list) across all infrastructure modules, which is consistent with the from __future__ import annotations already present in every file.

findings.summary: JSON and Markdown writes in generate_multi_project_summary_report have no error handling, unlike the consistent try/raise pattern in pipeline_reporter.py
evidence: multi_project_reporter.py lines 170-180: json.dump and f.write calls are bare — no try/except, no logger.error, no raise on OSError
pipeline_reporter.py lines 116-123: identical I/O path uses try/except OSError with logger.error and re-raise
The same operation (write JSON to disk) uses two different error strategies in the same package
findings.suggestion: Wrap the bare json.dump and f.write calls in generate_multi_project_summary_report with try/except OSError that logs and re-raises, matching the established pattern in pipeline_reporter.py.

findings.summary: generate_multi_project_report catches bare Exception, logs, and re-raises without wrapping original context
evidence: Lines 60-62: 'except Exception as e: logger.error(...); raise' — the raise re-raises the original exception unmodified, which is acceptable, but the bare Exception catch masks structural errors (e.g., AttributeError in generate_executive_summary) as generic reporting failures with no added context
The rest of the codebase uses specific exception types (OSError, TypeError, KeyError) at catch sites
findings.suggestion: Narrow the except clause to the exceptions that can actually be raised by the called functions, or wrap in a domain-specific exception (e.g., TemplateError) to preserve context at the boundary.

findings.summary: retry_on_transient_failure is a pure forwarding wrapper with no added policy over retry_with_backoff
evidence: Lines 135-162: retry_on_transient_failure does nothing except call retry_with_backoff(max_attempts=max_attempts, initial_delay=initial_delay, exceptions=TRANSIENT_EXCEPTIONS)
It only forwards two of retry_with_backoff's six parameters and exposes no unique policy (no different defaults for max_delay, exponential_base, jitter, or on_retry)
A caller could pass exceptions=TRANSIENT_EXCEPTIONS directly to retry_with_backoff with the same result
findings.suggestion: Either remove retry_on_transient_failure and have callers pass exceptions=TRANSIENT_EXCEPTIONS, or give it a distinct policy (e.g., fixed max_delay=10.0 or jitter=False) that justifies its existence as a named variant.

findings.summary: RetryableOperation context manager provides a second retry mechanism with no clear differentiation from the decorator, no callers visible in reviewed files
evidence: Lines 165-264: RetryableOperation implements __enter__, __exit__, __iter__, __next__, succeed(), retry() — a full manual retry protocol
The module docstring describes the decorator pattern as the primary API; the context manager is a parallel mechanism covering the same use case
No callers of RetryableOperation were found in reviewed files; only the decorator form (retry_with_backoff) is imported in pipeline.py and other modules
findings.suggestion: If RetryableOperation has no active callers, remove it. If it serves a real use case requiring manual control, document the specific scenario that the decorator cannot handle.
